en.extract:
The black-and-white mannikin (Spermestes bicolor) also black-and-white munia or red-backed mannikin, is a species of estrildid finch, widely present throughout sub-Saharan Africa. It has an estimated global extent of occurrence of 4,200,000 km2 (1,600,000 sq mi).
It is found in moist savanna and subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest habitat. The status of the species is evaluated as least concern. They are seedeaters, but are known to feed on algae.
The black-and-white mannikin was formally described in 1843 by the British zoologist and collector Louis Fraser from a specimen collected near Cape Palmas in Liberia. He placed the species in the genus Amadina and coined the binomial name Amadina bicolor. The black-and-white mannikin is now one of the four species placed in the genus Spermestes that was introduced in 1837 by William Swainson.
Four subspecies are recognised:

S. b. bicolor (Fraser, 1843) – Guinea-Bissau to Cameroon
S. b. poensis (Fraser, 1843) – south Cameroon to south Sudan, southwest Ethiopia, west Kenya and the island of Bioko
S. b. woltersi (Schouteden, 1956) – southeast DR Congo and northwest Zambia
S. b. nigriceps Cassin, 1852 – central Kenya and south Somalia to east Angola, Zambia, Zimbabwe and east South Africa
The subspecies S. b. woltersi and S. b. nigriceps have sometimes been considered as a separate species, the red-backed mannikin.


References

The '''black-and-white mannikin''' ('''''Spermestes bicolor''''') also '''black-and-white munia''' or '''red-backed mannikin''', is a species of [[estrildid finch]], widely present throughout [[sub-Saharan Africa]]. It has an estimated global extent of occurrence of {{cvt|4200000|km2}}.
It is found in moist [[savanna]] and [[subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest]] habitat. The status of the species is evaluated as [[least concern]]. They are seedeaters, but are known to feed on algae.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Savalli | first=U.M. | date=1989 | title=Black and white mannikins ''Lonchura bicolor'' eating algae | journal=Scopus | volume=13 | page=136 | url=https://archive.org/details/biostor-135221/mode/1up}}</ref>
 
The black-and-white mannikin was [[species description|formally described]] in 1843 by the British zoologist and collector [[Louis Fraser]] from a specimen collected near [[Cape Palmas]] in Liberia. He placed the species in the genus ''[[Amadina]]'' and coined the [[binomial name]] ''Amadina bicolor''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Fraser | first=Louis | author-link=Louis Fraser | date=1842 | title=''Amadina bicolor'' | journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London | volume=10 | issue=117 | page=145 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/12862332 }} Although the volume is dated 1842 on the title page, the article was not published until 1843.</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Paynter | editor-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1968 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=14 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | location=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=371 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14481572 }}</ref> The black-and-white mannikin is now one of the four species placed in the genus ''[[Spermestes]]'' that was introduced in 1837 by [[William Swainson]].<ref>{{ cite book | last=Swainson | first=William | author-link=William Swainson | year=1837 | title=On the Natural History and Classification of Birds | volume=2 | location=London  | publisher=John Taylor | page=280 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/41945058 }}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=January 2023 | title=Waxbills, parrotfinches, munias, whydahs, Olive Warbler, accentors, pipits | work=IOC World Bird List Version 13.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/waxbills/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=10 February 2023 }}</ref>

Four [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=ioc/>
* ''S. b. bicolor'' ([[Louis Fraser|Fraser]], 1843) – Guinea-Bissau to Cameroon
* ''S. b. poensis'' (Fraser, 1843) – south Cameroon to south Sudan, southwest Ethiopia, west Kenya and the island of [[Bioko]]
* ''S. b. woltersi'' ([[Henri Schouteden|Schouteden]], 1956) – southeast DR Congo and northwest Zambia
* ''S. b. nigriceps'' [[John Cassin|Cassin]], 1852 – central Kenya and south Somalia to east Angola, Zambia, Zimbabwe and east South Africa

The subspecies ''S. b. woltersi'' and ''S. b. nigriceps'' have sometimes been considered as a separate species, the red-backed mannikin.<ref name=ioc/><ref name=bow>{{cite taxon|BOW| last=Payne | first=R.B. | year=2020 | title=Black-and-white Mannikin (''Spermestes bicolor'') | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/bawman1/1.0/introduction | version= 1.0 | doi=10.2173/bow.bawman1.01}}</ref>

zh.extract:
黑白文鸟（学名：Spermestes bicolor），是梅花雀科文鸟属的一种，分布于撒哈拉以南非洲。全球活动范围约为3,880,000平方千米。该物种的保护状况被评为无危。
种名 bicolor 意为“二色的”。
黑白文鸟的平均体重约为8.5克。栖息地包括亚热带或热带的（低地）干燥疏灌丛、牧草地、亚热带或热带的湿润低地林、亚热带或热带的旱林、耕地、湿地和干燥的稀树草原。


参考文献
